  cat("This is the slowest step in the CIM section. Please wait.\n")

  set.seed(678)
  z020.perm.cim <- cim(
    cross      = z020,
    pheno.col  = "PlantHeight",
    method     = "hk",
    n.marcovar = 27,
    window     = Inf,
    n.perm     = 1000
  )

  save(z020.perm.cim, file = rdata_perm_cim)
  cat("CIM permutations complete. Saved to:", rdata_perm_cim, "\n")
}

cim_threshold <- summary(z020.perm.cim, alpha = 0.05)
cat("\n--- CIM LOD threshold at alpha = 0.05 ---\n")
print(cim_threshold)
```

```{r cim-apply-threshold}
# applying the LOD 6.24 threshold to both window scans to see which
# peaks are genuinely significant rather than just locally tall
#
# summary() with perms= only returns positions clearing the
# threshold, max one row per chromosome (the highest peak that
# clears it)

cat("=== Significant QTL from CIM, window = 15 ===\n")
cim15_sig <- summary(z020.cim15, perms = z020.perm.cim, alpha = 0.05)
print(cim15_sig)

cat("\n=== Significant QTL from CIM, window = Inf ===\n")
cimInf_sig <- summary(z020.cimInf, perms = z020.perm.cim, alpha = 0.05)
print(cimInf_sig)

save(cim15_sig, cimInf_sig, cim_threshold,
     file = "data/cim_significant.RData")
cat("\nSaved to data/cim_significant.RData\n")
```

```{r cim-plot, fig.cap="Figure 12. Composite interval mapping LOD profiles across all 10 linkage groups, comparing window = 15 cM (orange) and window = infinite (cyan) cofactor exclusion settings. The dashed red line marks the genome-wide significance threshold (LOD 6.24, alpha = 0.05). Green ticks mark positions of marker cofactors selected for the window = 15 scan."}
# both CIM window scans overlaid, threshold drawn as a horizontal
# line, green ticks via add.cim.covar() showing where the window = 15
# cofactors were picked - comparing window settings visually makes it
# obvious which peaks are stable vs sensitive to the window choice

plot(
  z020.cim15, z020.cimInf,
  col  = c("orange", "cyan"),
  main = "Composite Interval Mapping -- Plant Height"
)

add.threshold(
  z020.cim15,
  perms = z020.perm.cim,
  alpha = 0.05,
  col   = "firebrick",
  lty   = 2
)

add.cim.covar(z020.cim15, col = "darkgreen")

legend(
  "topright",
  legend = c("window = 15", "window = Inf", "Threshold (LOD 6.24)"),
  lty    = c(1, 1, 2),
  col    = c("orange", "cyan", "firebrick"),
  box.col = NA
)
```

```{r cim-makeqtl}
# building the QTL object from the 3 CIM-confirmed positions (LG4,
# LG6, LG8), using the window = Inf positions since that's the
# stricter scan and its peaks are a touch more stable (no
# same-chromosome cofactor can nudge them)
#
# sim.geno() re-run here so the imputation draws match the current
# genoprob grid - same n.draws = 32, step = 1 as the IM fitqtl step

z020 <- sim.geno(z020, n.draws = 32, step = 1)

cim_chr <- cimInf_sig$chr
cim_pos <- cimInf_sig$pos

cat("Building QTL object at:\n")
for (i in seq_along(cim_chr)) {
  cat("  Q", i, ": LG", cim_chr[i], " at ", round(cim_pos[i], 1),
      " cM (LOD ", round(cimInf_sig$lod[i], 2), ")\n", sep = "")
}

z020.cim.qtl <- makeqtl(z020, chr = cim_chr, pos = cim_pos)
print(z020.cim.qtl)

save(z020.cim.qtl, cim_chr, cim_pos, file = "data/cim_qtl_object.RData")
cat("\nSaved to data/cim_qtl_object.RData\n")
```

```{r cim-fitqtl-combined}
# fitting all 3 QTL together rather than one at a time:
#
#   - joint %var properly accounts for any shared variance between
#     QTL instead of double-counting overlap from 3 separate
#     single-QTL fits
#   - the drop-one-at-a-time table shows what each QTL contributes
#     ABOVE the other two, a fairer measure of independent
#     importance than a stand-alone effect
#
# formula = y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3, additive only, no interaction term -
# epistasis testing is MIM's job later, not CIM's

z020.cim.fit <- fitqtl(
  z020,
  qtl       = z020.cim.qtl,
  formula   = y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3,
  get.ests  = TRUE,
  pheno.col = "PlantHeight"
)

cat("=== Combined three-QTL model (CIM) ===\n\n")
print(summary(z020.cim.fit))

save(z020.cim.fit, file = "data/cim_fitqtl.RData")
cat("\nSaved to data/cim_fitqtl.RData\n")
```

```{r cim-vs-im-plot, fig.cap="Figure 13. LOD profiles for linkage groups 4, 6, and 8 comparing interval mapping (red, EM method) and composite interval mapping with an infinite window (cyan). Dashed lines show the genome-wide significance threshold for each method (IM: red dashed; CIM: cyan dashed). Green ticks mark cofactor marker positions selected for composite interval mapping."}
# IM vs CIM, restricted to the 3 chromosomes CIM confirmed (LG4, 6,
# 8) - cutting the rest of the genome out of the plot makes the
# sharpening effect of CIM much easier to actually see
#
# using z020.em for IM (exact ML, the method treated as primary IM
# result) and z020.cimInf for CIM (stricter window, the one the QTL
# model above is built from)

plot(
  z020.em, z020.cimInf,
  chr  = c(4, 6, 8),
  col  = c("red", "cyan"),
  main = "Interval Mapping versus Composite Interval Mapping"
)

add.threshold(
  z020.em,
  perms = z020.perm.em,
  alpha = 0.05,
  col   = "red",
  lty   = 2
)

add.threshold(
  z020.cimInf,
  perms = z020.perm.cim,
  alpha = 0.05,
  col   = "cyan",
  lty   = 2
)

add.cim.covar(z020.cimInf, chr = c(4, 6, 8), col = "darkgreen")

legend(
  "topleft",
  legend  = c("IM (EM method)", "CIM (window = Inf)",
              "IM threshold", "CIM threshold"),
  lty     = c(1, 1, 2, 2),
  col     = c("red", "cyan", "red", "cyan"),
  box.col = NA
)
```

## Composite Interval Mapping Results Summary

Composite interval mapping confirmed three QTL for plant height,
all consistent across two cofactor exclusion window settings
(15 cM and infinite). The strongest QTL, on linkage group 6 at
108 cM, showed a marked increase in LOD score relative to single
marker analysis and interval mapping (6.62, 7.34, and 17.97
respectively), indicating that controlling for background genetic
variation substantially sharpened this signal. A new QTL on linkage
group 8 was detected only under composite interval mapping,
illustrating the method's ability to resolve moderate-effect loci
that are masked by larger-effect QTL elsewhere in the genome under
simpler mapping approaches. Conversely, a marginally significant
signal on linkage group 9 detected under interval mapping did not
hold up under composite interval mapping, suggesting it reflected
uncontrolled background variation rather than an independent QTL.
Jointly, the three confirmed QTL (linkage groups 4, 6, and 8)
explained 33.87% of phenotypic variance in plant height. At every
position, the NC350 allele was associated with greater plant
height, consistent with the direction of effect observed throughout
single marker analysis and interval mapping.

---

## Multiple Interval Mapping

```{r mim-load-and-check}
# MIM picks up directly from CIM - z020 already has calc.genoprob()
# and sim.geno() attached, no new cross object or map needed
#
# just confirming the CIM objects are still around before going
# further (skipping the professor's summaryMap/plotMap/plotRF checks
# here since the map was already verified back in the linkage mapping
# section, no need to repeat that)

library(qtl)

stopifnot(exists("z020"))
stopifnot(exists("z020.cim.qtl"))   # the 3-QTL object from CIM (LG4, 6, 8)
stopifnot(exists("z020.cim.fit"))   # the fitqtl() result from CIM

cat("Starting Multiple Interval Mapping.\n")
cat("Carrying forward CIM-confirmed QTL: LG4, LG6, LG8\n\n")

print(z020.cim.qtl)
```

```{r mim-refineqtl}
# refineqtl() re-optimises each QTL position one at a time while
# holding the others fixed - a QTL's best position can shift slightly
# once the others are properly accounted for jointly, which CIM's
# cofactor approach doesn't quite do (cofactors control for
# background variance but don't jointly optimise QTL positions
# against each other)
#
# iterates through Q1/Q2/Q3 testing small shifts, repeats until no
# more LOD gain, prints its own progress as it goes

z020.ref <- refineqtl(
  cross     = z020,
  pheno.col = "PlantHeight",
  qtl       = z020.cim.qtl,
  formula   = y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3
)

cat("\nRefined QTL positions:\n")
print(z020.ref)

save(z020.ref, file = "data/mim_ref1.RData")
cat("\nSaved to data/mim_ref1.RData\n")
```

```{r mim-scantwo-permutations}
# scantwo() tests every PAIR of genome positions jointly, not single
# positions like scanone/cim - needed here for two things:
#
#   1. penalised LOD thresholds (via permutation) for proper model
#      selection - without this there's no principled way to decide
#      whether one more QTL, or one more interaction term, actually
#      earns its place in the model
#   2. later, scanning the WHOLE genome for epistasis, including
#      pairs where neither locus is significant as a main effect on
#      its own
#
# WARNING: this is the slowest step in the whole project by a wide
# margin - every pair of positions, x 1000 permuted datasets. can run
# for hours. cached to disk like the other slow steps. do NOT
# interrupt this once it starts or it has to begin again from scratch

rdata_perm_two <- "data/mim_scantwo_perm.RData"

if (file.exists(rdata_perm_two)) {

  load(rdata_perm_two)
  cat("Loaded cached scantwo permutation results from:",
      rdata_perm_two, "\n")

} else {

  cat("Running 1000 scantwo permutations. This is the slowest step\n")
  cat("in the whole project and may take several hours.\n")
  cat("Do not close R or restart your session while this runs.\n\n")

  set.seed(678)
  z020.perm.two <- scantwo(
    z020,
    pheno.col = "PlantHeight",
    method    = "hk",
    n.perm    = 1000
  )

  save(z020.perm.two, file = rdata_perm_two)
  cat("\nscantwo permutations complete. Saved to:", rdata_perm_two, "\n")
}

cat("\n--- scantwo permutation thresholds (alpha = 0.05) ---\n")
print(summary(z020.perm.two, alpha = 0.05))
```

```{r mim-fitqtl-refined}
# re-fitting the 3-QTL model at the REFINED positions (LG4 @ 67,
# LG6 @ 108, LG8 @ 126) rather than the original CIM peaks - since
# positions moved slightly under refineqtl(), the test stats need
# recalculating at the new positions

z020.fit.ref <- fitqtl(
  z020,
  pheno.col = "PlantHeight",
  qtl       = z020.ref,
  formula   = y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3,
  get.ests  = TRUE
)

cat("=== Refined three-QTL model ===\n\n")
print(summary(z020.fit.ref))

save(z020.fit.ref, file = "data/mim_fitqtl_ref1.RData")
cat("\nSaved to data/mim_fitqtl_ref1.RData\n")
```

```{r mim-addqtl-round1}
# addqtl() scans the genome asking: if a QTL got added HERE, on top
# of the 3 already in the model, how much would the model improve?
# different from a plain scan since the existing 3 QTL are held in
# as covariates the whole time, so this is really hunting for a
# fourth, independent signal rather than just re-finding the same 3
# peaks
#
# comparing the top hit against the av1 threshold (2.55) from the
# scantwo permutations - that's the threshold calibrated specifically
# for "is one more additive QTL worth adding to an existing model"

z020.add1 <- addqtl(
  z020,
  pheno.col = "PlantHeight",
  qtl       = z020.ref,
  formula   = y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3
)

cat("=== addqtl scan: searching for a 4th QTL ===\n\n")
cat("Top candidate position per chromosome:\n")
print(summary(z020.add1))

cat("\nOverall maximum:\n")
print(max(z020.add1))

cat("\nav1 threshold (from scantwo permutations):", 2.55, "\n")

top_add1_lod <- max(z020.add1)$lod
if (top_add1_lod > 2.55) {
  cat("Result: LOD", round(top_add1_lod, 2),
      "exceeds the threshold. This position is a candidate to add.\n")
} else {
  cat("Result: LOD", round(top_add1_lod, 2),
      "does not exceed the threshold. No further QTL to add.\n")
}

save(z020.add1, file = "data/mim_addqtl1.RData")
cat("\nSaved to data/mim_addqtl1.RData\n")
```

```{r mim-add-fourth-qtl}
# adding the LG10 candidate (102 cM) to the 3-QTL model and
# refining again - a new QTL can shift where the others sit best,
# so always refine right after adding
#
# NOTE: max(z020.add1)$chr comes back as an R factor. c() converts
# factors to their internal INTEGER CODE rather than the text label
# when mixed with a character vector - this silently turned
# chromosome "10" into "1" the first time I ran this, caught it by
# checking the printed chromosome against what addqtl actually
# reported. as.character() wrapped around the factor fixes it, kept
# here as a standing reminder for the rest of the addqtl rounds below

chr <- c(as.character(max(z020.add1)$chr), z020.ref$chr)
pos <- c(max(z020.add1)$pos, z020.ref$pos)

cat("Building 4-QTL object at:\n")
for (i in seq_along(chr)) {
  cat("  Q", i, ": LG", chr[i], " at ", round(pos[i], 1), " cM\n", sep = "")
}

z020.qtl2 <- makeqtl(z020, chr = chr, pos = pos)
print(z020.qtl2)

z020.ref2 <- refineqtl(
  z020,
  pheno.col = "PlantHeight",
  qtl       = z020.qtl2,
  formula   = y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3 + Q4
)

cat("\nRefined 4-QTL positions:\n")
print(z020.ref2)

save(z020.qtl2, z020.ref2, file = "data/mim_qtl2_ref2.RData")
cat("\nSaved to data/mim_qtl2_ref2.RData\n")
```

```{r mim-fitqtl-4qtl}
# fitting the 4-QTL model (LG4, LG6, LG8, LG10) - this is also where
# the LG10 sign gets checked against the antagonistic marker
# PZA02386.2 flagged back in SMA, where B73 was associated with
# taller plants, opposite to every other significant marker at the
# time

z020.fit2 <- fitqtl(
  z020,
  pheno.col = "PlantHeight",
  qtl       = z020.ref2,
  formula   = y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3 + Q4,
  get.ests  = TRUE
)

cat("=== Four-QTL model (LG4, LG6, LG8, LG10) ===\n\n")
print(summary(z020.fit2))

save(z020.fit2, file = "data/mim_fitqtl2.RData")
cat("\nSaved to data/mim_fitqtl2.RData\n")
```

```{r mim-addqtl-round2}
# second addqtl() round, now conditional on all 4 confirmed QTL -
# checking for a 5th independent signal, same logic as round 1

z020.add2 <- addqtl(
  z020,
  pheno.col = "PlantHeight",
  qtl       = z020.ref2,
  formula   = y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3 + Q4
)

cat("=== addqtl scan: searching for a 5th QTL ===\n\n")
print(summary(z020.add2))

cat("\nOverall maximum:\n")
print(max(z020.add2))

cat("\nav1 threshold (from scantwo permutations):", 2.55, "\n")

top_add2_lod <- max(z020.add2)$lod
if (top_add2_lod > 2.55) {
  cat("Result: LOD", round(top_add2_lod, 2),
      "exceeds the threshold. This position is a candidate to add.\n")
} else {
  cat("Result: LOD", round(top_add2_lod, 2),
      "does not exceed the threshold. Stop here, no further QTL.\n")
}

save(z020.add2, file = "data/mim_addqtl2.RData")
cat("\nSaved to data/mim_addqtl2.RData\n")
```

```{r mim-add-fifth-qtl}
# adding the LG5 candidate (13 cM, LOD 2.65) - worth flagging this is
# a marginal addition, only just over the 2.55 threshold, not as
# clear-cut as LG4/LG6/LG8/LG10 were
#
# as.character() applied to the chromosome value again, same factor
# coercion issue as before, not taking any chances with it a second
# time

chr <- c(as.character(max(z020.add2)$chr), z020.ref2$chr)
pos <- c(max(z020.add2)$pos, z020.ref2$pos)

cat("Building 5-QTL object at:\n")
for (i in seq_along(chr)) {
  cat("  Q", i, ": LG", chr[i], " at ", round(pos[i], 1), " cM\n", sep = "")
}

z020.qtl3 <- makeqtl(z020, chr = chr, pos = pos)
print(z020.qtl3)

z020.ref3 <- refineqtl(
  z020,
  pheno.col = "PlantHeight",
  qtl       = z020.qtl3,
  formula   = y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3 + Q4 + Q5
)

cat("\nRefined 5-QTL positions:\n")
print(z020.ref3)

save(z020.qtl3, z020.ref3, file = "data/mim_qtl3_ref3.RData")
cat("\nSaved to data/mim_qtl3_ref3.RData\n")
```

```{r mim-fitqtl-5qtl}
# fitting the 5-QTL model (LG4, LG5, LG6, LG8, LG10) - checking
# whether LG5 holds up as genuinely significant once properly fitted
# jointly, given it came in as a marginal addqtl() hit, and checking
# its effect size/direction relative to the other four

z020.fit3 <- fitqtl(
  z020,
  pheno.col = "PlantHeight",
  qtl       = z020.ref3,
  formula   = y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3 + Q4 + Q5,
  get.ests  = TRUE
)

cat("=== Five-QTL model (LG4, LG5, LG6, LG8, LG10) ===\n\n")
print(summary(z020.fit3))

save(z020.fit3, file = "data/mim_fitqtl3.RData")
cat("\nSaved to data/mim_fitqtl3.RData\n")
```

```{r mim-addqtl-round3}
# third addqtl() round, conditional on all 5 confirmed QTL - checking
# whether a 6th turns up, or whether this is where the manual search
# naturally stops

z020.add3 <- addqtl(
  z020,
  pheno.col = "PlantHeight",
  qtl       = z020.ref3,
  formula   = y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3 + Q4 + Q5
)

cat("=== addqtl scan: searching for a 6th QTL ===\n\n")
print(summary(z020.add3))

cat("\nOverall maximum:\n")
print(max(z020.add3))

cat("\nav1 threshold (from scantwo permutations):", 2.55, "\n")

top_add3_lod <- max(z020.add3)$lod
if (top_add3_lod > 2.55) {
  cat("Result: LOD", round(top_add3_lod, 2),
      "exceeds the threshold. This position is a candidate to add.\n")
} else {
  cat("Result: LOD", round(top_add3_lod, 2),
      "does not exceed the threshold. STOP. The manual search ends",
      "at five QTL.\n")
}

save(z020.add3, file = "data/mim_addqtl3.RData")
cat("\nSaved to data/mim_addqtl3.RData\n")
```

```{r mim-calc-penalties}
# stepwiseqtl() needs the penalty values in a specific format, not
# the summary table printed earlier - calc.penalties() converts the
# scantwo permutation results into the three values it actually uses:
#
#   main  : penalty per extra QTL (main effect)
#   heavy : interaction penalty, from the "full" threshold, used
#           comparing a 2-QTL interacting model against no QTL
#   light : interaction penalty, from the "fv1" threshold, used
#           comparing a 2-QTL interacting model against the best
#           single-QTL model
#
# these let stepwiseqtl() weigh whether a QTL or an interaction
# genuinely earns its complexity, same logic as the av1 threshold
# used by hand throughout the addqtl() rounds above

penalties <- calc.penalties(z020.perm.two)

cat("Penalties for stepwiseqtl():\n")
print(penalties)

save(penalties, file = "data/mim_penalties.RData")
cat("\nSaved to data/mim_penalties.RData\n")
```

```{r mim-stepwiseqtl}
# stepwiseqtl() runs an automatic forward/backward search: starts
# from no QTL, adds the best position, keeps adding (refining
# positions each time) as long as pLOD keeps improving, then works
# backward checking whether dropping any QTL still leaves an equal or
# better pLOD
#
# this is the independent cross-check against the manual search - if
# they agree, good confirmation the manual process found the right
# answer. if they disagree, that's worth digging into rather than
# brushing aside
#
# max.qtl = 8 gives some headroom above the manual 5-QTL result in
# case the automatic search lands somewhere different

z020.step <- stepwiseqtl(
  z020,
  pheno.col = "PlantHeight",
  max.qtl   = 8,
  method    = "hk",
  penalties = penalties
)

cat("\n=== stepwiseqtl() final model ===\n\n")
print(z020.step)

save(z020.step, file = "data/mim_stepwiseqtl.RData")
cat("\nSaved to data/mim_stepwiseqtl.RData\n")
```

```{r mim-addint}
# addint() tests every pairwise interaction among the QTL already in
# the model (LG4, LG5, LG6, LG8, LG10), asking whether adding that
# specific interaction term improves the model beyond the two main
# effects alone
#
# narrower test than the genome-wide scantwo() scan below - this only
# checks pairs among QTL already known, can't find an interacting
# partner with no main effect of its own
#
# relevant threshold here is "int" (4.01) from the scantwo
# permutations, since this is testing the interaction term
# specifically

z020.addint <- addint(
  z020,
  pheno.col = "PlantHeight",
  qtl       = z020.ref3,
  formula   = y ~ Q1 + Q2 + Q3 + Q4 + Q5
)

cat("=== Pairwise interaction tests among the 5 confirmed QTL ===\n\n")
print(z020.addint)

cat("\nint threshold (from scantwo permutations): 4.01\n")
max_int_lod <- max(z020.addint$LOD)
cat("Highest interaction LOD found:", round(max_int_lod, 3), "\n")

if (max_int_lod > 4.01) {
  cat("Result: at least one interaction exceeds the threshold.\n")
} else {
  cat("Result: no interaction among the 5 confirmed QTL exceeds the",
      "threshold. No evidence of epistasis among these specific loci.\n")
}

save(z020.addint, file = "data/mim_addint.RData")
cat("\nSaved to data/mim_addint.RData\n")
```

```{r mim-scantwo-actual}
# the scantwo permutation chunk above only computed the permuted null
# distribution - the scan on the REAL (non-permuted) data is a
# separate run, needed to actually identify interacting pairs once
# compared against the permutation thresholds
#
# cached to disk like the other slow steps, though this should be
# noticeably quicker than the 1000-permutation run since it's one
# scan, not a thousand

rdata_scantwo_actual <- "data/mim_scantwo_actual.RData"

if (file.exists(rdata_scantwo_actual)) {

  load(rdata_scantwo_actual)
  cat("Loaded cached scantwo actual-data results from:",
      rdata_scantwo_actual, "\n")

} else {

  cat("Running scantwo() on the actual data (single scan, not",
      "permuted). This should take a few minutes.\n")

  z020.two <- scantwo(
    z020,
    pheno.col = "PlantHeight",
    method    = "hk"
  )

  save(z020.two, file = rdata_scantwo_actual)
  cat("Saved to:", rdata_scantwo_actual, "\n")
}

cat("\n=== Genome-wide two-QTL scan summary ===\n")
cat("(showing pairs with notable full-model or interaction LOD)\n\n")
print(summary(z020.two))

save(z020.two, file = "data/mim_scantwo_actual.RData")
```

```{r mim-plot-lod-profile, fig.cap="Figure 14. LOD profile for the final five-QTL multiple interval mapping model, showing the profile around each QTL position with all other QTL held fixed in the model. Peak height and sharpness reflect each QTL's relative contribution and positional resolution."}
# plotLodProfile() shows the LOD profile around each QTL's position
# with the other four held fixed in the model - different from a
# plain genome scan since it shows how sharply each position is
# resolved GIVEN the rest of the model is already accounted for,
# which is the more relevant view once a multi-QTL model exists
#
# a narrow sharp peak = well-resolved position. a broad flat one
# usually points to lower marker density or a smaller effect size in
# that region

plotLodProfile(
  z020.ref3,
  main = "LOD Profile for Final 5-QTL Model -- Plant Height"
)
```

## Multiple Interval Mapping Results Summary

Multiple interval mapping built on the three QTL confirmed by
composite interval mapping, identifying two additional loci through
iterative scanning: linkage group 10 at 102 cM and linkage group 5
at 13 cM. The final model of five QTL explained 42.31% of phenotypic
variance in plant height, with the linkage group 6 and linkage
group 4 loci contributing the largest individual effects. An
independent automatic search confirmed linkage groups 4, 6, and 10
as the most robustly supported QTL, while linkage groups 5 and 8
were retained by the manual search as additional, more marginal
loci. The linkage group 5 QTL was notable for showing the opposite
direction of effect to all other loci in the model, with the B73
allele rather than the NC350 allele associated with greater plant
height. No evidence of epistatic interaction was found between any
pair of QTL, either among the five loci in the final model or
genome-wide, indicating that plant height in this population is
controlled by a small number of additive QTL of varying effect
size, consistent with the moderate-effect polygenic architecture
suggested by the trait's continuous distribution.
